note: Kristy-isms — one-line, in-voice nutrition education. Each is tied to a trigger so it appears contextually, never randomly. Fixed editorial copy (not model-generated); the claim lock governs the model note, not these. None state a user diagnosis or a treatment claim.

isms.text: Banned in the EU, legal here. That's a choice someone made — not a safety guarantee.
If you can't pronounce it, your body probably can't either.
'Vegetable oil' doesn't come from vegetables. It comes from seeds — under heat and solvent.
Sugar wears about 60 different names on labels. Maltodextrin and dextrose are two of them.
That color isn't food. It's petroleum in a costume.
Most sodium was never in your salt shaker. It's already in the box.
'Natural flavor' can legally mean almost anything. Real strawberry flavor comes from strawberries.
A few ingredients, all real. This is what food used to look like.
Emulsifiers keep things smooth on the shelf — and rough on your gut lining.
'Enriched' means they stripped the nutrition out, then added a fraction back.
If it never goes bad, it's worth asking what's keeping it alive.
The longer the ingredient list, the further it is from food.
Read the back, not the front. The front is marketing; the back is the truth.
Shop the edges of the store. The real food lives on the perimeter.
The best foods don't have an ingredient list. They are the ingredient.
